name: Structure and function of proteins and their constituent amino acids

nodes.label: Amino Acids
nodes.summary: Amino acids are the monomeric building blocks of proteins, each with an amino group, carboxyl group, hydrogen, and variable R-group on a central alpha-carbon.

nodes.label: General Amino Acid Structure
nodes.summary: All standard amino acids share a common backbone differing only in their side chain (R-group).

nodes.label: Alpha-carbon
nodes.summary: The alpha-carbon is the central carbon bonded to the amino group, carboxyl group, a hydrogen, and the R-group.

nodes.label: Amino group
nodes.summary: The amino group (-NH2/-NH3+) is the basic nitrogen-containing functional group attached to the alpha-carbon.

nodes.label: Carboxyl group
nodes.summary: The carboxyl group (-COOH/-COO-) is the acidic functional group attached to the alpha-carbon.

nodes.label: Side chain (R-group)
nodes.summary: The R-group is the variable side chain that determines each amino acid's chemical properties.

nodes.label: The 20 standard amino acids
nodes.summary: Proteins are built from a common set of 20 standard genetically encoded alpha-amino acids, each with a distinct side chain.

nodes.label: Three- and one-letter abbreviations
nodes.summary: Each amino acid has standard three-letter (e.g., Ala) and one-letter (e.g., A) abbreviations used to write sequences.

nodes.label: Amphoteric character
nodes.summary: Amino acids are amphoteric: the carboxyl can donate a proton (acid) and the amino group can accept one (base), so they react as both acid and base.

nodes.label: Zwitterion
nodes.summary: At physiological pH an amino acid exists as a zwitterion with a protonated amino group and deprotonated carboxyl group, giving net charge near zero.

nodes.label: Dipolar nature
nodes.summary: Amino acids carry both positive and negative charges simultaneously, making them dipolar and highly water-soluble.

nodes.label: Amino Acid Stereochemistry
nodes.summary: With the exception of glycine, amino acids are chiral and predominantly the L-configuration in proteins.

nodes.label: Chirality of amino acids
nodes.summary: The alpha-carbon is a stereocenter (four different groups) in all amino acids except glycine.

nodes.label: Glycine is achiral
nodes.summary: Glycine has two hydrogens on the alpha-carbon, so it is the only standard amino acid that is achiral.

nodes.label: L-amino acids in proteins
nodes.summary: Essentially all amino acids found in proteins are of the L-configuration.

nodes.label: D/L vs R/S designation
nodes.summary: L-amino acids correspond to S configuration except cysteine, which is R due to the sulfur in its side chain changing CIP priority.

nodes.label: Absolute configuration (Fischer)
nodes.summary: In a Fischer projection of an L-amino acid the amino group points to the left with the carboxyl at top.

nodes.label: D/L relative to glyceraldehyde
nodes.summary: The D/L system assigns configuration by relating the amino acid's stereocenter to L- or D-glyceraldehyde as a reference.

nodes.label: D-amino acids (rare biological roles)
nodes.summary: D-amino acids are rare in nature, appearing in bacterial cell walls and some peptide antibiotics, but not in ribosomally synthesized proteins.

nodes.label: Amino Acid Classification
nodes.summary: The 20 standard amino acids are grouped by side-chain chemistry: nonpolar, polar uncharged, acidic, and basic.

nodes.label: Nonpolar (hydrophobic) side chains
nodes.summary: Nonpolar amino acids (Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Met, Trp) have hydrophobic side chains that cluster in protein interiors.

nodes.label: Polar uncharged side chains
nodes.summary: Polar uncharged amino acids (Ser, Thr, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln) have side chains that form hydrogen bonds without a formal charge at pH 7.

nodes.label: Acidic (negatively charged) side chains
nodes.summary: Aspartate and glutamate have carboxylic-acid side chains that are deprotonated and negatively charged at physiological pH.

nodes.label: Basic (positively charged) side chains
nodes.summary: Lysine, arginine, and histidine have basic side chains that are protonated and positively charged at physiological pH (His only partially).

nodes.label: Acidic side-chain pKa values
nodes.summary: Asp (~3.7) and Glu (~4.2) side-chain carboxyls have low pKa values and carry negative charge above those pH values.

nodes.label: Basic side-chain pKa values
nodes.summary: Lys (~10.5), Arg (~12.5), and His (~6) side chains differ in pKa, setting the pH range over which each carries positive charge.

nodes.label: Aromatic side chains
nodes.summary: Phenylalanine, tyrosine, and tryptophan contain aromatic rings and absorb UV light near 280 nm.

nodes.label: Sulfur-containing amino acids
nodes.summary: Cysteine and methionine contain sulfur; cysteine can form disulfide bonds and methionine is the start-codon residue.

nodes.label: Hydroxyl-containing amino acids
nodes.summary: Serine, threonine, and tyrosine bear hydroxyl groups that hydrogen-bond and serve as phosphorylation and glycosylation sites.

nodes.label: Amide-containing amino acids
nodes.summary: Asparagine and glutamine carry uncharged amide side chains, the polar derivatives of aspartate and glutamate.

nodes.label: Essential amino acids
nodes.summary: Essential amino acids cannot be synthesized by humans and must be obtained from the diet.

nodes.label: Hydrophobicity / hydropathy
nodes.summary: A residue's hydropathy index predicts whether it buries in the hydrophobic core or faces aqueous solvent.

nodes.label: Special-property residues
nodes.summary: Several amino acids have unique structural roles arising from their distinctive side chains.

nodes.label: Proline
nodes.summary: Proline's side chain loops back to the backbone nitrogen forming a rigid ring that introduces kinks and disrupts helices/sheets.

nodes.label: Proline is a secondary amine (imino acid)
nodes.summary: Proline's nitrogen is part of its ring, making it a secondary amine (imino acid) that cannot donate a backbone amide hydrogen for hydrogen bonding.

nodes.label: Glycine flexibility
nodes.summary: Glycine's single-hydrogen side chain gives the backbone maximal conformational flexibility and allows tight turns.

nodes.label: Cysteine and disulfide bonds
nodes.summary: Two cysteine thiols can be oxidized to form a covalent disulfide (cystine) bridge that stabilizes tertiary/quaternary structure.

nodes.label: Histidine pKa near physiological pH
nodes.summary: Histidine's imidazole side chain has a pKa near 6, letting it act as a proton donor/acceptor in enzyme active sites and buffer near pH 7.

nodes.label: Methionine as the start residue
nodes.summary: Methionine is encoded by the AUG start codon, so it is the first residue incorporated into nearly all eukaryotic polypeptides.

nodes.label: Ser/Thr/Tyr phosphorylation sites
nodes.summary: The hydroxyl groups of serine, threonine, and tyrosine are phosphorylated by kinases to switch protein activity on or off.

nodes.label: Amino Acid Acid-Base Chemistry
nodes.summary: Amino acids are polyprotic, with characteristic pKa values that govern charge as a function of pH.

nodes.label: Characteristic pKa values
nodes.summary: Typical pKa values are ~2 for the alpha-carboxyl, ~9-10 for the alpha-amino, plus side-chain pKa values for ionizable R-groups.

nodes.label: Polyprotic / stepwise deprotonation
nodes.summary: Amino acids have multiple ionizable groups that gain and lose protons stepwise, from most protonated at low pH to least protonated at high pH.

nodes.label: Isoelectric point (pI)
nodes.summary: The pI is the pH at which an amino acid carries no net charge, computed as the average of the two pKa values flanking the neutral species.

nodes.label: Calculating pI
nodes.summary: For amino acids with an ionizable side chain, pI is the average of the two pKa values bracketing the zwitterionic form.

nodes.label: pI of acidic and basic amino acids
nodes.summary: Acidic amino acids have low pI values and basic amino acids have high pI values because the side-chain pKa shifts which two pKas flank neutrality.

nodes.label: Titration curve
nodes.summary: An amino acid titration curve shows stepwise deprotonation with buffering plateaus at each pKa and net charge changing with pH.

nodes.label: Henderson-Hasselbalch application
nodes.summary: pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]) predicts the protonation state of each ionizable group at a given pH.

nodes.label: Net charge at given pH
nodes.summary: Net charge is determined by comparing the solution pH to each group's pKa: groups with pKa above pH are protonated.

nodes.label: Buffering near pKa
nodes.summary: An ionizable group buffers most effectively at pH values within about one unit of its pKa.

nodes.label: Peptide Bond Formation
nodes.summary: Amino acids link via peptide (amide) bonds to form peptides and proteins.

nodes.label: Peptide Bond Formation
nodes.summary: A peptide bond forms between the carboxyl of one amino acid and the amino group of the next.

nodes.label: Condensation (dehydration) reaction
nodes.summary: Peptide bond formation is a condensation reaction that releases one water molecule.

nodes.label: Amide linkage
nodes.summary: The peptide bond is an amide bond joining a carbonyl carbon to a backbone nitrogen.

nodes.label: Peptide bond hydrolysis
nodes.summary: Peptide bonds are cleaved by hydrolysis, adding water across the amide bond, often catalyzed by proteases.

nodes.label: N-terminus and C-terminus
nodes.summary: Polypeptides have a free amino N-terminus (written first) and a free carboxyl C-terminus (written last).

nodes.label: Directionality of synthesis
nodes.summary: Proteins are synthesized and conventionally read from the N-terminus to the C-terminus.

nodes.label: Residue and peptide nomenclature
nodes.summary: Once joined, each amino acid unit is a residue; chains are named dipeptide, oligopeptide, or polypeptide by length.

nodes.label: Polypeptide backbone
nodes.summary: The repeating N-Calpha-C(=O) sequence forms the polypeptide backbone from which side chains project.

nodes.label: Peptide Bond Properties
nodes.summary: The peptide bond has partial double-bond character that constrains backbone geometry.

nodes.label: Resonance / partial double-bond character
nodes.summary: Resonance delocalizes electrons between the carbonyl oxygen and the C-N bond, giving the peptide bond partial double-bond character.

nodes.label: Planarity and rigidity
nodes.summary: Partial double-bond character makes the peptide bond planar and rigid, with no free rotation about the C-N bond.

nodes.label: Trans configuration preference
nodes.summary: Peptide bonds favor the trans configuration to minimize steric clash, except in some X-proline bonds.

nodes.label: Phi and psi dihedral angles
nodes.summary: Rotation is permitted about the N-Calpha (phi) and Calpha-C (psi) bonds, defining backbone conformation.

nodes.label: Ramachandran plot
nodes.summary: A Ramachandran plot maps sterically allowed phi/psi combinations, clustering at alpha-helix and beta-sheet regions.

nodes.label: Protein Structure (Hierarchy)
nodes.summary: Protein structure is organized into four hierarchical levels: primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary.

nodes.label: Primary Structure
nodes.summary: Primary structure is the linear amino acid sequence linked by peptide bonds.

nodes.label: Amino acid sequence
nodes.summary: Primary structure is the covalently linked order of amino acids encoded by the gene.

nodes.label: Stabilized by covalent bonds only
nodes.summary: Primary structure is held together solely by covalent peptide bonds (and disulfides), unlike the noncovalent higher levels.

nodes.label: Sequence determines folding
nodes.summary: The amino acid sequence dictates the protein's higher-order structure and ultimately its function.

nodes.label: Effect of sequence changes
nodes.summary: A single amino acid substitution can drastically alter protein structure and function, as in sickle-cell hemoglobin.

nodes.label: Secondary Structure
nodes.summary: Secondary structure consists of local backbone conformations stabilized by hydrogen bonds.

nodes.label: Alpha-helix
nodes.summary: The alpha-helix is a right-handed coil stabilized by backbone hydrogen bonds between residues spaced four apart (i to i+4).

nodes.label: Alpha-helix geometry (3.6 residues/turn)
nodes.summary: The alpha-helix has ~3.6 residues per turn with side chains projecting outward and the helix axis running through the coil.

nodes.label: Beta-pleated sheet
nodes.summary: Beta-sheets form from extended strands hydrogen-bonded side by side, in parallel or antiparallel orientation.

nodes.label: Backbone hydrogen bonding
nodes.summary: Secondary structure is stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone carbonyl oxygens and amide hydrogens.

nodes.label: Beta-turn / reverse turn
nodes.summary: Beta-turns reverse the polypeptide direction over a few residues, often involving glycine and proline.

nodes.label: Helix breakers (Pro, Gly)
nodes.summary: Proline kinks and glycine's flexibility disrupt regular alpha-helices and beta-sheets.

nodes.label: Parallel vs antiparallel sheets
nodes.summary: Antiparallel beta-sheets have linear hydrogen bonds and are more stable than the slanted bonds of parallel sheets.

nodes.label: Side chains not involved in stabilization
nodes.summary: Secondary structure depends on backbone hydrogen bonds, not side-chain interactions, which is why it is sequence-independent in principle.

nodes.label: Supersecondary structure / motifs
nodes.summary: Recurring combinations of secondary-structure elements form motifs that recur across many proteins.

nodes.label: Structural motifs
nodes.summary: Motifs such as helix-turn-helix, beta-barrel, and coiled-coil are recurring arrangements of alpha-helices and beta-sheets.

nodes.label: Tertiary Structure
nodes.summary: Tertiary structure is the overall three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide stabilized by side-chain interactions.

nodes.label: Three-dimensional fold
nodes.summary: Tertiary structure is the global 3D arrangement of all atoms in a single polypeptide chain.

nodes.label: Side-chain (R-group) interactions
nodes.summary: Tertiary structure is determined by interactions among side chains, in contrast to the backbone hydrogen bonds of secondary structure.

nodes.label: Hydrophobic interactions
nodes.summary: Burial of nonpolar side chains away from water is the dominant driving force of tertiary folding.

nodes.label: Disulfide bonds
nodes.summary: Covalent disulfide bridges between cysteines covalently lock tertiary (and quaternary) structure.

nodes.label: Side-chain hydrogen bonds
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonds between polar side chains help stabilize the folded tertiary structure.

nodes.label: Salt bridges (ionic interactions)
nodes.summary: Electrostatic attractions between oppositely charged side chains (salt bridges) stabilize tertiary structure.

nodes.label: Van der Waals / London forces
nodes.summary: Weak transient van der Waals interactions among tightly packed atoms contribute to fold stability.

nodes.label: Hydrophobic core / polar surface
nodes.summary: In globular proteins nonpolar residues pack the interior core while polar/charged residues face the aqueous surface.

nodes.label: Protein domains
nodes.summary: Domains are independently folding, often functionally distinct modules within a polypeptide.

nodes.label: Quaternary Structure
nodes.summary: Quaternary structure is the assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits into a functional complex.

nodes.label: Subunit assembly
nodes.summary: Quaternary structure arises when two or more folded polypeptide subunits assemble into one functional protein.

nodes.label: Stabilized by the same noncovalent forces
nodes.summary: Subunits are held together by the same hydrophobic, hydrogen-bonding, ionic, and sometimes disulfide interactions that stabilize tertiary structure.

nodes.label: Hemoglobin as quaternary example
nodes.summary: Hemoglobin is a classic quaternary protein with two alpha and two beta subunits, each binding a heme.

nodes.label: Subunit cooperativity
nodes.summary: Quaternary structure enables cooperative behavior, such as hemoglobin's sigmoidal oxygen-binding curve.

nodes.label: Stabilizing Forces (Summary)
nodes.summary: A defined set of covalent and noncovalent forces stabilizes the higher-order structure of proteins.

nodes.label: Covalent vs noncovalent forces
nodes.summary: Disulfide bonds are covalent, while hydrogen bonds, ionic, hydrophobic, and van der Waals interactions are noncovalent.

nodes.label: Entropy and the hydrophobic effect
nodes.summary: The hydrophobic effect is entropically driven by releasing ordered water from around nonpolar surfaces.

nodes.label: Which force stabilizes which level
nodes.summary: Primary = peptide bonds; secondary = backbone hydrogen bonds; tertiary/quaternary = side-chain hydrophobic, ionic, hydrogen-bond, and disulfide interactions.

nodes.label: Protein Folding and Denaturation
nodes.summary: Proteins fold into a native conformation that can be lost (denatured) and sometimes regained.

nodes.label: Native State and Folding
nodes.summary: The native state is the functional, lowest-free-energy conformation of a protein.

nodes.label: Native conformation
nodes.summary: The native conformation is the biologically active, thermodynamically favored folded form.

nodes.label: Anfinsen / thermodynamic hypothesis
nodes.summary: Anfinsen showed that primary sequence alone can specify the native fold, which is the global free-energy minimum.

nodes.label: Folding funnel / Levinthal's paradox
nodes.summary: Folding follows an energy funnel toward the native state rather than a random search, resolving Levinthal's paradox of too-many conformations.

nodes.label: Solvation and water's role
nodes.summary: Folding is driven largely by the aqueous environment, with water excluding nonpolar surfaces and ordering the hydrophobic core.

nodes.label: Chaperone proteins
nodes.summary: Molecular chaperones assist correct folding and prevent aggregation but do not change the final native structure.

nodes.label: Denaturation
nodes.summary: Denaturation disrupts higher-order structure while leaving the primary sequence intact.

nodes.label: Denaturation definition
nodes.summary: Denaturation is loss of secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure without breaking peptide bonds.

nodes.label: Denaturation abolishes function
nodes.summary: Because function depends on 3D shape, denaturation typically destroys biological activity even though the sequence is intact.

nodes.label: Heat denaturation
nodes.summary: Heat disrupts noncovalent interactions, unfolding the protein and abolishing function.

nodes.label: pH-induced denaturation
nodes.summary: Extreme pH alters side-chain charges, disrupting ionic interactions and hydrogen bonds.

nodes.label: Chemical denaturants
nodes.summary: Urea, guanidinium chloride, and detergents disrupt hydrophobic and hydrogen-bonding interactions.

nodes.label: Reducing agents break disulfides
nodes.summary: Reducing agents like beta-mercaptoethanol or DTT cleave disulfide bonds, disrupting structure.

nodes.label: Renaturation / refolding
nodes.summary: Some denatured proteins refold spontaneously to the native state when denaturing conditions are removed.

nodes.label: Misfolding and Aggregation
nodes.summary: Improper folding can produce nonfunctional or pathological protein aggregates.

nodes.label: Protein aggregation
nodes.summary: Misfolded proteins can expose hydrophobic patches and aggregate, sometimes forming amyloid fibrils.

nodes.label: Prions
nodes.summary: Prions are misfolded proteins that template misfolding of normal copies, causing disease.

nodes.label: Non-Enzymatic Protein Function
nodes.summary: Proteins perform diverse non-catalytic roles including structure, transport, motility, signaling, immune defense, and binding.

nodes.label: Structural Proteins
nodes.summary: Structural proteins provide mechanical support and shape to cells and tissues.

nodes.label: Collagen
nodes.summary: Collagen is a triple-helix structural protein rich in glycine and proline that provides tensile strength to connective tissue.

nodes.label: Elastin
nodes.summary: Elastin gives tissues elasticity, allowing them to stretch and recoil.

nodes.label: Keratin
nodes.summary: Keratin is a fibrous structural protein, rich in disulfide bonds, found in hair, nails, and skin.

nodes.label: Tubulin and actin (cytoskeleton)
nodes.summary: Tubulin (microtubules) and actin (microfilaments) are cytoskeletal structural proteins that also serve as tracks for motor proteins.

nodes.label: Fibrous vs globular proteins
nodes.summary: Fibrous proteins are elongated and insoluble for structure, while globular proteins are compact and soluble for dynamic functions.

nodes.label: Motor Proteins
nodes.summary: Motor proteins convert chemical energy into mechanical movement along cytoskeletal tracks.

nodes.label: Myosin
nodes.summary: Myosin is a motor protein that walks along actin filaments using ATP hydrolysis to drive muscle contraction.

nodes.label: Kinesin and dynein
nodes.summary: Kinesin and dynein are motor proteins that transport cargo along microtubules in opposite directions.

nodes.label: Actin filaments as tracks
nodes.summary: Actin polymerizes into microfilaments that serve as tracks for myosin and provide cell motility.

nodes.label: ATP hydrolysis powers movement
nodes.summary: Motor proteins couple cycles of ATP binding and hydrolysis to conformational changes that produce directed force.

nodes.label: Binding and Transport Proteins
nodes.summary: Many proteins function by reversibly binding ligands or transporting molecules.

nodes.label: Hemoglobin oxygen transport
nodes.summary: Hemoglobin reversibly binds oxygen with cooperative, sigmoidal kinetics for efficient transport.

nodes.label: Myoglobin oxygen storage
nodes.summary: Myoglobin is a single-subunit oxygen-storage protein with hyperbolic, high-affinity binding.

nodes.label: Ligand binding specificity
nodes.summary: Binding proteins recognize specific ligands through complementary shape and noncovalent interactions.

nodes.label: Binding affinity and reversibility
nodes.summary: Binding is described by an equilibrium dissociation constant; most physiological binding is reversible and tunable.

nodes.label: Membrane transport proteins
nodes.summary: Channel and carrier proteins move ions and molecules across membranes.

nodes.label: Transport / carrier proteins (e.g., albumin)
nodes.summary: Soluble carrier proteins such as serum albumin bind and shuttle hydrophobic molecules, drugs, and ions through the blood.

nodes.label: Signaling and Recognition Proteins
nodes.summary: Proteins mediate cell signaling, immune recognition, and hormonal control.

nodes.label: Receptor proteins
nodes.summary: Receptor proteins bind signaling molecules and transduce signals across membranes.

nodes.label: Antibodies (immunoglobulins)
nodes.summary: Antibodies are immune proteins that bind antigens with high specificity through variable regions.

nodes.label: Peptide hormones
nodes.summary: Peptide and protein hormones such as insulin act as extracellular chemical messengers.

nodes.label: G proteins
nodes.summary: G proteins are GTP-binding switch proteins that relay signals from receptors to effectors.

nodes.label: Immune and Recognition Function
nodes.summary: Proteins mediate immune defense and cell-cell recognition.

nodes.label: Antibody structure (heavy/light chains)
nodes.summary: Antibodies have two heavy and two light chains held by disulfide bonds, with antigen-binding variable regions and constant regions.

nodes.label: MHC and antigen presentation
nodes.summary: Major histocompatibility complex proteins present peptide antigens on the cell surface for immune recognition.

nodes.label: Cell Adhesion and Clotting Proteins
nodes.summary: Proteins mediate cell-cell adhesion and form structural networks such as blood clots.

nodes.label: Cell adhesion molecules
nodes.summary: Cell adhesion molecules such as cadherins, integrins, and selectins bind cells to each other and to the extracellular matrix.

nodes.label: Fibrinogen / fibrin (clotting)
nodes.summary: Fibrinogen is cleaved to fibrin, which polymerizes into the structural mesh of a blood clot.

nodes.label: Conjugated and Modified Proteins
nodes.summary: Many proteins are covalently or noncovalently modified with non-amino-acid groups that tune their function.

nodes.label: Conjugated Protein Types
nodes.summary: Conjugated proteins are classified by the chemical identity of their attached prosthetic group.

nodes.label: Glycoproteins
nodes.summary: Glycoproteins carry covalently attached carbohydrate groups important for recognition and stability.

nodes.label: Lipoproteins
nodes.summary: Lipoproteins associate proteins with lipids to transport hydrophobic molecules in the blood.

nodes.label: Metalloproteins
nodes.summary: Metalloproteins contain a bound metal ion essential to their structure or catalytic function.

nodes.label: Hemoproteins / chromoproteins
nodes.summary: Hemoproteins (e.g., hemoglobin, cytochromes) carry a heme prosthetic group; chromoproteins carry a colored prosthetic group.

nodes.label: Nucleoproteins and phosphoproteins
nodes.summary: Nucleoproteins (e.g., histones) bind nucleic acids; phosphoproteins carry covalently bound phosphate groups.

nodes.label: Prosthetic groups
nodes.summary: A prosthetic group is a tightly bound non-amino-acid component required for protein activity, such as heme.

nodes.label: Apoprotein vs holoprotein
nodes.summary: An apoprotein lacks its prosthetic group and is inactive; the complete, active form with the group bound is the holoprotein.

nodes.label: Protein Separation and Analysis
nodes.summary: Proteins are isolated and characterized by techniques exploiting size, charge, and binding properties.

nodes.label: Electrophoresis
nodes.summary: Electrophoresis separates proteins in an electric field based on charge and size.

nodes.label: Electrophoresis principle (charge in a field)
nodes.summary: Charged proteins migrate in an electric field toward the oppositely charged electrode, with mobility set by charge, size, and shape.

nodes.label: SDS-PAGE
nodes.summary: SDS-PAGE denatures proteins and coats them with negative charge so they separate by size alone.

nodes.label: Native PAGE
nodes.summary: Native PAGE separates folded proteins by size, shape, and charge without denaturation.

nodes.label: Isoelectric focusing
nodes.summary: Isoelectric focusing separates proteins along a pH gradient where each migrates to its pI and stops.

nodes.label: 2D gel electrophoresis
nodes.summary: 2D gels combine isoelectric focusing then SDS-PAGE to resolve proteins by pI and size.

nodes.label: Chromatography
nodes.summary: Chromatography separates proteins by partitioning between a stationary and mobile phase.

nodes.label: Chromatography principle
nodes.summary: Proteins separate by differential partitioning or interaction between a stationary phase and a flowing mobile phase.

nodes.label: Size-exclusion chromatography
nodes.summary: Size-exclusion (gel-filtration) chromatography separates by size, eluting larger proteins first.

nodes.label: Ion-exchange chromatography
nodes.summary: Ion-exchange chromatography separates proteins by net charge using a charged stationary phase.

nodes.label: Affinity chromatography
nodes.summary: Affinity chromatography isolates a protein by its specific binding to an immobilized ligand.

nodes.label: Quantification and Identification
nodes.summary: Protein concentration and identity are determined by spectroscopic and immunological assays.

nodes.label: UV absorbance at 280 nm
nodes.summary: Aromatic residues absorb UV near 280 nm, allowing protein concentration estimation via Beer's law.

nodes.label: Colorimetric assays (Bradford, BCA)
nodes.summary: Bradford and BCA assays produce a color change proportional to protein concentration.

nodes.label: Western blotting
nodes.summary: Western blots detect a specific protein using antibodies after gel separation and transfer.

nodes.label: ELISA
nodes.summary: ELISA quantifies a specific protein using an antibody linked to an enzyme that generates a measurable color signal.

nodes.label: Edman degradation
nodes.summary: Edman degradation sequentially removes and identifies the N-terminal residue to determine sequence.

nodes.label: Mass spectrometry
nodes.summary: Mass spectrometry identifies proteins and peptides by their mass-to-charge ratio.

nodes.label: X-ray crystallography / structure determination
nodes.summary: X-ray crystallography and NMR determine the atomic-resolution three-dimensional structure of a protein.

external_prereqs.concept_hint: Acid-base equilibria, pH, pKa, and the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation
Acid-base titration curves and equivalence points
Stereochemistry: absolute configuration and R/S Cahn-Ingold-Prelog priority rules
D/L relative configuration referenced to glyceraldehyde
Intermolecular forces and the hydrophobic effect
Entropy, free energy, and spontaneity of processes
Movement of charged particles in an electric field
Point mutations and the genetic code (e.g., sickle-cell missense mutation)
AUG start codon and translation initiation
